stages.title: Phase 0: The Block System
stages.desc: What Hangul blocks are, before seeing any letters

steps.title: Welcome to Hangul!
steps.desc: Korean doesn't write letters in a line like English. It stacks letters into BLOCKS. Each block = one syllable. You'll understand the system before seeing a single letter.
goals: Understand what a syllable block is
Learn the reading direction inside a block

steps.exampleEn: Each square you see in Korean text is one syllable
steps.note: Think of each block as a small square. INSIDE the square are slots for consonants and vowels. The block is the basic unit of Korean writing, not individual letters.

steps.example: Every block has slots for its parts
steps.note: The simplest block is just C+V. The BOTTOM slot comes later.

steps.q: Inside a block, what is the reading direction?
opts: Right to left, bottom to top
Left to right, top to bottom
steps.ans: Left to right, top to bottom
steps.hint: Same as English reading order, but inside one square

opts: One syllable made of assembled parts
steps.ans: One syllable made of assembled parts
steps.hint: Blocks are assembled from consonant + vowel parts

steps.title: Blocks Have Parts
steps.text:
Common mistake: treating a block as a single picture.
Always look INSIDE for its parts:
• Start consonant (always present)
• Vowel (determines block shape)
• BOTTOM consonant (optional: 받침)

stages.title: Phase 1: Core Vowels
stages.desc: 6 base vowels: the shapes that determine how every block is built

steps.title: Your first vowels
steps.desc: You'll learn 6 vowels as 3 contrast pairs. Each pair is a mirror or rotation. Learn one, get the other free. This lesson: the two 'line' vowels.
goals: Know which is VERTICAL vs HORIZONTAL family

steps.note: This is the simplest vowel: one VERTICAL line. It belongs to the VERTICAL family. Later it will sit to the RIGHT of a consonant.

steps.phonetic: no English equivalent, like a tight 'uh'
steps.note: One HORIZONTAL line. It belongs to the HORIZONTAL family. Later it will sit BELOW a consonant. Opposite orientation from ㅣ.

steps.q: Which vowel is a VERTICAL line?
The vowel that sounds like 'eu' (a tight uh): which family does it belong to?
steps.hint: Think about the shape of the vowel for this sound

steps.q: The vowel that sounds like 'i' (as in see): which family does it belong to?
steps.hint: Think about the shape of the vowel for this sound

steps.title: 4 more vowels
steps.desc: Two more contrast pairs. Each pair differs ONLY by which direction the short stroke points.
goals: Recognize ㅏ vs ㅓ (stroke RIGHT vs LEFT)
Recognize ㅗ vs ㅜ (stroke UP vs DOWN)

steps.exampleEn: VERTICAL line + short stroke pointing RIGHT
steps.note: ㅏ = ㅣ with a short stroke pointing RIGHT. VERTICAL family. Sits to the right of a consonant.

steps.exampleEn: VERTICAL line + short stroke pointing LEFT
steps.note: ㅓ = ㅣ with a short stroke pointing LEFT. Also VERTICAL family. The ONLY difference from ㅏ is the stroke direction. This is the #1 mirror trap for beginners.

steps.q: The Korean vowel for the sound 'a' (as in 'ah') has its short stroke pointing…
steps.hint: Picture the vowel shape in your mind

steps.exampleEn: HORIZONTAL line + short stroke pointing UP
steps.note: ㅗ = ㅡ with a short stroke pointing UP. HORIZONTAL family. Sits BELOW a consonant.

steps.exampleEn: HORIZONTAL line + short stroke pointing DOWN
steps.note: ㅜ = ㅡ with a short stroke pointing DOWN. Also HORIZONTAL family. Mirror of ㅗ.

steps.q: The Korean vowel for the sound 'eo' (an open uh) has its short stroke pointing…
steps.hint: It is the mirror of the 'a' vowel

steps.q: Which of these are HORIZONTAL family?
steps.hint: HORIZONTAL = based on the ㅡ line

steps.q: The Korean vowel for the sound 'o' (as in oh) has its short stroke pointing…
steps.hint: Picture the HORIZONTAL-family vowel for this sound

steps.q: VERTICAL family vowels sit ___ of the consonant later.
steps.hint: VERTICAL spine → right side of block

steps.title: Mirror Trap Drill
steps.text:
Always check the SHORT stroke direction:
• ㅏ / ㅓ = Right vs Left
• ㅗ / ㅜ = Up vs Down
Never guess. Always check.

stages.title: Phase 2: Silent ㅇ & First Blocks
stages.desc: Your first real Hangul blocks using ㅇ as the empty starter

steps.title: Every block needs a consonant
steps.desc: Korean rule: every syllable block MUST start with a consonant. But what about syllables that start with a vowel sound? That's where ㅇ comes in.
goals: Learn that ㅇ is SILENT at the start

steps.exampleEn: ㅇ fills the start slot when there's no consonant sound
steps.note: ㅇ in the Start slot has ZERO sound. It's just a slot-filler so the block has a consonant in the required position. Don't say 'ng' here. That sound only happens when ㅇ is at the BOTTOM of a block (you'll learn that later).

steps.q: When ㅇ is in the START slot of a block, it sounds like…
Why does Korean use ㅇ at the start?
opts: Every block must start with a consonant
steps.ans: Every block must start with a consonant
steps.hint: Think about what the Start slot requires

steps.title: Building with ㅇ + vowels
steps.desc: Now combine ㅇ with each of the 6 core vowels to make your first real Korean syllable blocks.
goals: Know which use LEFT placement vs TOP placement

steps.exampleEn: VERTICAL vowel → ㅇ on the left, vowel on the right
steps.note: 아 = 'a'. Since ㅏ is a VERTICAL vowel, ㅇ goes on the LEFT.

steps.exampleEn: HORIZONTAL vowel → ㅇ on TOP, vowel BELOW
steps.note: 오 = 'o'. Since ㅗ is a HORIZONTAL vowel, ㅇ goes on TOP.

steps.q: You want to write the sound 'a'. The vowel ㅏ is VERTICAL. Where does ㅇ go?
steps.hint: VERTICAL vowels → consonant goes on the LEFT

steps.q: You want to write the sound 'o'. The vowel ㅗ is HORIZONTAL. Where does the consonant go?
steps.hint: HORIZONTAL vowels → consonant goes on TOP

steps.q: You want to write the sound 'i'. The vowel ㅣ is VERTICAL. Where does ㅇ go?
steps.hint: VERTICAL family = consonant on the left

steps.q: You want to write the sound 'eo'. The vowel ㅓ is VERTICAL. Where does ㅇ go?
steps.hint: VERTICAL vowel → consonant on the left

steps.q: You want to write the sound 'u'. The vowel ㅜ is HORIZONTAL. Where does ㅇ go?
What is the rule for HORIZONTAL vowels (ㅗ, ㅜ, ㅡ)?
steps.hint: Think: HORIZONTAL line BELOW, consonant ABOVE

steps.title: Two ㅇ Rules
steps.text:
• Never write a vowel alone: ㅏ is wrong, 아 is correct
• INITIAL ㅇ is SILENT (not 'ng')
• BOTTOM ㅇ = 'ng' sound
Always use ㅇ as the SILENT placeholder at the start.

stages.title: Phase 3: First Consonants
stages.desc: ㄱ ㄴ ㅁ ㅅ: build real blocks with all 6 vowels

steps.title: Your first real consonants
steps.desc: Time to replace ㅇ with real consonants. These 4 are visually simple and high-frequency.

steps.example: 가 거 고 구 그 기
steps.exampleEn: ga geo go gu geu gi
steps.note: ㄱ looks like a reversed L, or the number 7. The angle opens toward the bottom-left.

steps.hint: The consonant shaped like a reversed L
What sound does the consonant make? Add the 'a' vowel.

steps.example: 나 너 노 누 느 니
steps.exampleEn: na neo no nu neu ni
steps.note: ㄴ looks like an L. The angle opens toward the top-right. Easy to remember: ㄴ = L = 'n' sound.

steps.hint: Identify the consonant, then the vowel.
Which consonant is at the TOP? What vowel is BELOW it?

steps.title: Two more consonants
steps.desc: ㅁ (square) and ㅅ (tent/hat). After this lesson you know 4 consonants + ㅇ + 6 vowels. Enough for many syllables!

steps.example: 마 머 모 무 므 미
steps.exampleEn: ma meo mo mu meu mi
steps.note: ㅁ is a square. Don't confuse it with ㅇ (circle). Look at the corners. ㅁ has 4 sharp corners; ㅇ is round.

steps.example: 사 서 소 수 스 시
steps.exampleEn: sa seo so su seu si
steps.note: ㅅ looks like a tent or hat shape. Note: 시 sounds closer to 'shi' than 'si'. Learn the Korean sound, not the romanization.

stages.title: Phase 4: More Consonants
stages.desc: ㄷ ㅂ ㅈ ㅎ: expanding your inventory, with confusion drills

steps.title: Consonants 5 & 6
steps.desc: Same block rules. No new structure, just new pieces. Focus on telling them apart from letters you already know.

steps.example: 다 더 도 두 드 디
steps.exampleEn: da deo do du deu di
steps.note: ㄷ has a flat top (like a table). Contrast: ㄱ has an open corner, ㄷ has a closed top line.

steps.hint: The consonant with a flat top like a table

steps.example: 바 버 보 부 브 비
steps.exampleEn: ba beo bo bu beu bi
steps.note: ㅂ looks boxy but has vertical legs hanging down. Key discrimination: ㅂ vs ㅁ: ㅂ has an open bottom with legs, ㅁ is a fully closed square.

steps.hint: The boxy consonant with legs hanging down

steps.title: Consonants 7 & 8
steps.desc: Two more consonants to complete your first set. After this you know 9 consonants (8 real + ㅇ) and 6 vowels.

steps.example: 자 저 조 주 즈 지
steps.exampleEn: ja jeo jo ju jeu ji
steps.note: ㅈ = ㅅ with an extra horizontal stroke on TOP. That's the ONLY difference: ㅅ (tent) → ㅈ (tent + bar). Always check for the extra line.

steps.hint: The tent shape with an extra bar on TOP

steps.example: 하 허 호 후 흐 히
steps.exampleEn: ha heo ho hu heu hi
steps.note: ㅎ has a distinctive shape. Aim for one clean syllable. Don't over-aspirate (no 'huh-a' for 하, just 'ha').

steps.hint: Which consonant is this? Add the 'o' vowel.
Plain tent (no bar) + 'i' vowel. Remember: sounds more like 'shi'.

stages.title: Phase 5: The Special ㄹ
stages.desc: ㄹ gets dedicated time. Plus reading multi-block items

steps.title: ㄹ: Its Own Category
steps.desc: ㄹ is high-frequency and unfamiliar to most learners. Don't force it into English 'r' or 'l'. It's its own sound. This phase exists to prevent fossilization.

steps.phonetic: between r and l. Its own category
steps.example: 라 러 로 루 르 리
steps.exampleEn: ra reo ro ru reu ri
steps.note: ㄹ has a complex, wavy shape (multiple strokes). Don't swap it randomly with ㄴ. Recognize ㄹ, map it to the Korean sound. Refinement comes later.

steps.hint: Careful: is this the wavy one or the L-shaped one?

steps.title: Multi-syllable items
steps.desc: Multiple blocks = multiple syllables. Read left to right, one block at a time. Don't skip blocks or guess!
goals: Read two-block items block by block

steps.exampleEn: Read one block at a time, then combine
steps.note: Focus on the skill, not the meaning.

steps.hint: Decode block by block: first block, then second block.
Block 1: which consonant + which vowel? Block 2: same process.

steps.q: How many syllable blocks are in a two-syllable word?

steps.hint: Tent consonant for block 1, reversed-L consonant for block 2.

steps.title: Decoding Strategy
steps.text:
If you catch yourself guessing, STOP.
• Go back to block 1
• Sound it out completely
• Then move to block 2
Speed comes from accuracy, not from rushing.

stages.title: Phase 6: Block Architecture & Batchim
stages.desc: How blocks are stacked, and adding the BOTTOM slot

steps.title: How Korean blocks are built
steps.desc: Every Korean syllable is a block. Each block has a consonant and a vowel as its core. Blocks come in a few shapes depending on how many parts they have and whether the vowel is VERTICAL or HORIZONTAL.

steps.example:
ㄱ + ㅏ → 가 (ga)
ㄴ + ㅓ → 너 (neo)
steps.note: VERTICAL vowels (ㅏ ㅓ ㅣ) sit to the RIGHT of the consonant.

steps.example:
ㄱ + ㅗ → 고 (go)
ㄴ + ㅜ → 누 (nu)
steps.note: HORIZONTAL vowels (ㅗ ㅜ ㅡ) sit BELOW the consonant.

steps.hint: Consonant on the left + VERTICAL vowel on the right
Consonant on TOP + HORIZONTAL vowel BELOW

steps.en: Same as above, plus a consonant at the BOTTOM
steps.example:
ㄱ + ㅏ + ㄴ → 간 (gan)
ㄴ + ㅜ + ㄴ → 눈 (nun)
steps.note: 받침 (batchim) = BOTTOM consonant. 가 (ga) → 간 (gan). The most common block type.

steps.en: Two consonants share the BOTTOM slot
steps.note: Rare but real. Usually only ONE of the two BOTTOM consonants is pronounced.

steps.q: A CV block has how many parts?
A CVC block has how many parts?
steps.hint: Consonant + Vowel + final Consonant at BOTTOM

steps.title: Your first final consonants
steps.desc: ㄴ and ㅁ as batchim. These are stable: they always sound exactly like you'd expect: ㄴ = n, ㅁ = m.
goals: Read CVC blocks with ㄴ final
Read CVC blocks with ㅁ final

steps.exampleEn: The ㄴ at the BOTTOM adds 'n' to the end
steps.note: 안 = 'an'. Compare: 아 = 'a'. The only difference is the ㄴ at the BOTTOM.

steps.hint: What's at the BOTTOM of the block?

steps.exampleEn: The ㅁ at the BOTTOM adds 'm' to the end
steps.note: 암 = 'am'. ㅁ final is just as stable as ㄴ. Always sounds like 'm'.

steps.hint: What consonant is at the BOTTOM?
Reversed-L consonant + 'i' vowel + square consonant at BOTTOM

steps.title: ㄹ as final + the ㅇ role-switch
steps.desc: ㄹ at the BOTTOM = 'l' sound. And the BIG one: ㅇ at the BOTTOM = 'ng'. Same letter, completely different role from ㅇ at the start (SILENT).
goals: Read CVC blocks with ㄹ final

steps.note: 알 = 'al'. ㄹ as batchim always sounds like 'l'. Now you know all 4 stable batchim: ㄴ(n), ㅁ(m), ㄹ(l), ㅇ(ng).

steps.hint: Square consonant + 'a' vowel + wavy consonant at BOTTOM
L-shaped consonant + 'o' vowel + what at BOTTOM?

steps.exampleEn: ㅇ at start = SILENT. ㅇ at BOTTOM = 'ng'!
steps.note: 앙 = 'ang'. The same letter ㅇ appears TWICE but does completely different things. TOP = SILENT placeholder. BOTTOM = 'ng' like in 'sing'. This trips up every beginner.

steps.hint: What does ㅇ sound like at the BOTTOM?
ㅇ at TOP = SILENT. ㅇ at BOTTOM = ?

steps.title: ㅇ Role Switch
steps.text:
• ㅇ at TOP of block = SILENT placeholder
• ㅇ at BOTTOM of block = 'ng' sound
This is non-negotiable. Drill until automatic.

stages.title: Phase 7: Complex Vowels
stages.desc: 11 compound vowels built from the base vowels you already know

steps.title: Two vowels, similar sound
steps.desc: ㅐ and ㅔ sound very similar in modern Korean. But they are DIFFERENT SPELLINGS. You must recognize both.
goals: Recognize ㅐ and ㅔ as separate vowels
Read syllables with ㅐ and ㅔ

steps.note: ㅐ = ㅏ + ㅣ combined into one vowel. VERTICAL family.
ㅔ = ㅓ + ㅣ combined. Also VERTICAL family. In speech these sound almost identical. In writing they are DIFFERENT.

steps.title: Add an extra stroke = 'y' version
steps.desc: 4 vowels that are 'y-versions' of base vowels. The pattern: add one extra short stroke.

steps.note: ㅑ = ㅏ with an extra stroke → 'ya'. ㅕ = ㅓ with an extra stroke → 'yeo'. Same mirror pair logic.
ㅛ = ㅗ with an extra stroke → 'yo'. ㅠ = ㅜ with an extra stroke → 'yu'.

steps.title: Two vowels combined
steps.desc: ㅘ ㅝ ㅚ ㅟ are combinations of ㅗ/ㅜ with another vowel.

steps.note: ㅘ = ㅗ+ㅏ → 'wa'. ㅝ = ㅜ+ㅓ → 'wo'.
ㅚ = ㅗ+ㅣ → 'oe'. ㅟ = ㅜ+ㅣ → 'wi'.

steps.title: The densest vowels
steps.desc: These are the rarest and most visually complex. Recognize them, but they appear infrequently.
goals: Recognize ㅢ ㅙ ㅞ ㅒ ㅖ

steps.exampleEn: ui. Used in the possessive particle
steps.note: ㅢ = ㅡ+ㅣ → 'ui'. Appears in 의 (possessive particle). Pronunciation varies. For now, just recognize the shape.
Rare vowels. ㅙ = ㅗ+ㅏ+ㅣ, ㅞ = ㅜ+ㅓ+ㅣ, ㅒ = ㅑ+ㅣ, ㅖ = ㅕ+ㅣ. Recognize them when you see them.

steps.hint: ㅘ + ㅣ combined into one vowel

stages.title: Phase 8: Aspirated Consonants
stages.desc: ㅋ ㅌ ㅍ ㅊ: each plain consonant has a stronger, breathy sibling

steps.title: Plain → Aspirated
steps.desc: Korean has 3 types for many consonants: plain, aspirated, tense. Aspirated = add an extra stroke to the plain consonant. Breathier, stronger release.

steps.example: 카 커 코 쿠 크 키
steps.exampleEn: ka keo ko ku keu ki
steps.note: ㅋ = ㄱ + extra stroke. Stronger than ㄱ. Think: ㄱ is 'g/k', ㅋ is a heavier 'k' with a puff of air.

steps.hint: This consonant looks like ㄱ with an extra stroke

steps.example: 타 터 토 투 트 티
steps.exampleEn: ta teo to tu teu ti
steps.note: ㅌ = ㄷ + extra stroke. Stronger than ㄷ. ㄷ is 'd/t', ㅌ is a heavier 't' with aspiration.

steps.hint: This consonant looks like ㄷ with an extra stroke

steps.title: The other two aspirated consonants
steps.desc: ㅍ = aspirated ㅂ. ㅊ = aspirated ㅈ. Same rule: add a stroke = aspirated.
goals: Read syllables with all 4 aspirated consonants

steps.example: 파 퍼 포 푸 프 피
steps.exampleEn: pa peo po pu peu pi
steps.note: ㅍ = ㅂ + extra stroke. ㅂ is 'b/p', ㅍ is a heavier 'p'.

steps.example: 차 처 초 추 츠 치
steps.exampleEn: cha cheo cho chu cheu chi
steps.note: ㅊ = ㅈ + extra stroke. ㅈ is 'j', ㅊ is 'ch'. This pair has the biggest sound gap.

steps.hint: Which aspirated consonant looks like ㄱ with an extra stroke?
Which aspirated consonant looks like ㅂ with an extra stroke?

stages.title: Phase 9: Tense Consonants
stages.desc: ㄲ ㄸ ㅃ ㅆ ㅉ: doubled consonants, tighter onset

steps.title: Doubled = Tense
steps.desc: Tense consonants are visually DOUBLED versions of plain ones. The sound is tighter and more explosive, NOT just louder. Don't confuse tense with aspirated.
goals: Distinguish plain vs aspirated vs tense

steps.example: 까 꺼 꼬 꾸 끄 끼
steps.exampleEn: kka kkeo kko kku kkeu kki
steps.note: ㄲ = doubled ㄱ. No puff of air (unlike aspirated ㅋ). Tighter throat, sharper onset. The three types: ㄱ (plain g/k), ㅋ (aspirated k), ㄲ (tense kk).

steps.example: 따 떠 또 뚜 뜨 띠
steps.exampleEn: tta tteo tto ttu tteu tti
steps.note: ㄸ = doubled ㄷ. Three types: ㄷ (plain d/t), ㅌ (aspirated t), ㄸ (tense tt).

steps.title: Three more tense consonants
steps.desc: ㅃ, ㅆ, and ㅉ complete the tense set. All 5 tense consonants are doubled versions of plain ones.

steps.example: 빠 뻐 뽀 뿌 쁘 삐
steps.exampleEn: ppa ppeo ppo ppu ppeu ppi
steps.note: ㅃ = doubled ㅂ. Three types: ㅂ (plain b/p), ㅍ (aspirated p), ㅃ (tense pp).

steps.example: 싸 써 쏘 쑤 쓰 씨
steps.exampleEn: ssa sseo sso ssu sseu ssi
steps.note: ㅆ = doubled ㅅ. Unlike the others, ㅅ has NO aspirated version: only plain (ㅅ) and tense (ㅆ).

steps.example: 짜 쩌 쪼 쭈 쯔 찌
steps.exampleEn: jja jjeo jjo jju jjeu jji
steps.note: ㅉ = doubled ㅈ. Three types: ㅈ (plain j), ㅊ (aspirated ch), ㅉ (tense jj).

stages.title: Phase 10: Sound Rules
stages.desc: The 7 final sounds, liaison, nasalization, and aspiration

steps.title: Batchim neutralization
steps.desc: Many different consonants at the bottom of a block all collapse into just 7 possible final sounds. This is the key to reading Korean aloud.
goals: Match batchim consonants to their actual pronunciation

steps.exampleEn: Many letters, but only 7 possible endings

rules.from: ㄷ ㅌ ㅅ ㅆ ㅈ ㅊ

steps.trigger: at syllable end (before pause or consonant)
steps.note: The [t] group is the biggest: 6 letters all become [t].

steps.hint: ㅅ at the bottom neutralizes to which of the 7 sounds?
ㅊ at the bottom neutralizes to the same group as ㅅ
ㅋ at the bottom neutralizes to [k]
ㅍ at the bottom neutralizes to [p]

steps.title: Batchim slides forward
steps.desc: When a block ends with batchim AND the next block starts with ㅇ (silent), the batchim sound 'moves' to become the next syllable's initial consonant. Extremely frequent.

steps.exampleEn: ㄱ from 국 slides to fill the ㅇ of 어
steps.note: The consonant slides forward to fill the empty ㅇ.

steps.q: 먹어 (eat): the ㄱ from 먹 meets ㅇ of 어. How is it pronounced?
steps.hint: The ㄱ slides forward into the next syllable

opts: The ㅁ batchim moved to the ㅇ-start of 악
steps.ans: The ㅁ batchim moved to the ㅇ-start of 악

steps.title: Two more reading rules
steps.desc: Nasalization: stops become nasals before ㄴ/ㅁ. Aspiration: batchim consonant + ㅎ as next initial = aspirated version.

steps.note: Before ㄴ or ㅁ, expect the nasal version.

steps.q: 합니다: ㅂ appears before ㄴ. What does ㅂ become?
학년: ㄱ appears before ㄴ. What does ㄱ become?

steps.note: ㅎ 'powers up' the preceding consonant.

steps.q: 막히다: ㄱ batchim + ㅎ initial. What sound?
steps.hint: ㄱ + ㅎ combines into the aspirated version of ㄱ

steps.q: 급하다: ㅂ batchim + ㅎ initial. What sound?
steps.hint: ㅂ + ㅎ combines into the aspirated version of ㅂ

steps.title: Sound Rules Cheat Sheet
steps.text:
Don't panic! Three rules cover 90%:
• 받침 + ㅇ = liaison (carry sound over)
• Stop + ㄴ / ㅁ = nasal shift
• Consonant + ㅎ = aspirated

stages.title: Short Vowels & CVC
stages.desc: Five short vowels in simple words

steps.title: Your first two vowels
steps.desc: Dutch has 5 short vowels. This lesson: short a /ɑ/ and short o /ɔ/ in CVC words (consonant-vowel-consonant).
goals: Blend CVC words with these vowels

steps.note: Short a /ɑ/ in a closed syllable. Single letter a + consonant at end = short.
Short o /ɔ/ in a closed syllable. Single letter o + consonant = short.

steps.q: The word 'kat' (cat) has which vowel sound?
The word 'bos' (forest) has which vowel sound?
Which is a CVC word with short a?
steps.hint: CVC = consonant + single vowel + consonant, no double letters

steps.q: Which is a CVC word with short o?
steps.hint: Single letter o + consonant at end

steps.title: Three more short vowels
steps.desc: Short e /ɛ/, short i /ɪ/, and short u /ʏ/. Short u has NO English equivalent. It is a rounded front vowel.

steps.note: Short e /ɛ/. Note: the d sounds like t at the end (final devoicing). Spelling keeps d.
Short i /ɪ/. Familiar for English speakers.
Short u /ʏ/ = rounded front vowel. Say 'i' (bit) with rounded lips.

steps.q: Which short vowel has NO English equivalent?
steps.hint: One of these requires rounding your lips differently
Letter u in a CVC word

steps.title: All five short vowels
steps.desc: Mix all five. Can you identify them in CVC words?

steps.q: All five short vowels occur in closed syllables. A closed syllable ends in a…

stages.title: Long Vowels & Syllable Logic
stages.desc: The Dutch spelling engine: open vs closed

steps.title: The most important rule in Dutch
steps.desc: Open syllable (ends in vowel) → long. Closed syllable (ends in consonant) → short. This one rule explains most of Dutch spelling.
goals: Predict vowel length from syllable type

steps.exampleEn: Open syllable: vowel at the end
steps.note: la- ends in a vowel. Open → LONG. One letter a is enough because the syllable signals length.

steps.exampleEn: Closed syllable: consonant at the end
steps.note: lak ends in a consonant. Closed → SHORT. Same letter a, different sound.

steps.q: A syllable ending in a vowel letter is…

steps.hint: Does 'bo' end in vowel or consonant?
Does 'bom' end in vowel or consonant?

steps.q: Same letter 'a' in both 'lak' and 'la-ken'. Why different sounds?

lessons.title: Double Vowels: aa, ee, oo, uu

steps.title: Forcing long in a closed syllable
steps.desc: Need a long vowel but the syllable is closed? Double the vowel: aa, ee, oo, uu.
goals: Know when to use double vowels
Understand: closed syllable needs a length signal

steps.example: maan: closed but LONG → use aa
steps.exampleEn: Double vowel forces the long sound
steps.note: Closed syllable (ends in n), but long vowel. The double aa overrides the closed-syllable default.

steps.example: boom: closed but LONG → use oo
steps.note: Ends in m (closed), but long /oː/. oo forces it.

steps.q: Why does 'maan' use aa instead of a?
steps.hint: What does a closed syllable normally signal?

steps.q: Which word has a LONG vowel in a closed syllable?
steps.hint: Look for a double vowel + consonant ending

steps.title: Vowel length changes meaning
steps.desc: Wrong length = different word. Practice distinguishing them.
goals: Identify short vs long in pairs

steps.hint: Why double if not for length?

stages.title: Consonant Combos
stages.desc: Digraphs ch/ng, clusters, the Dutch g

steps.title: Consonant digraphs
steps.desc: ch = one sound. ng = one sound. sch = s + ch. Digraphs are NEVER doubled.

steps.note: ch is a digraph: two letters, one sound /x/.
ng is a digraph: two letters, one nasal /ŋ/.

steps.hint: Two letters, but think about what you hear

steps.q: The word for 'night' contains which consonant digraph?
steps.hint: Which two letters = one sound in this word?

steps.q: Digraphs ch and ng are NEVER…
steps.hint: Already two letters. Doubling makes no sense

steps.title: Consonant clusters
steps.desc: Dutch allows 2-3 consonants at start, 2-4 at end. Blend them. Never insert a vowel. Also: the Dutch g sounds nothing like English g.

steps.exampleEn: CCC onset always starts with s
steps.note: Three consonants blended: s-t-r. Never 'se-te-raat'.

steps.exampleEn: NOT like English g at all
steps.note: The Dutch g is a throaty sound (fricative). Northern = hard/raspy. Southern = soft/gentle. Both correct.

steps.q: Three-consonant onsets in Dutch always begin with which letter?
steps.hint: Think about which consonant can start a cluster of three
It is NOT the English g sound

steps.q: Pronouncing 'bron' as 'be-ron' is an error called…
steps.hint: Adding a vowel between clustered consonants

stages.title: The Spelling Engine
stages.desc: Consonant doubling and vowel dropping

steps.title: Protecting short vowels
steps.desc: Adding -en/-er/-e to a short-vowel word opens the syllable → vowel becomes long (wrong!). Fix: double the consonant.

steps.example: man + en → *ma-nen would open syllable → wrong
steps.exampleEn: Double n: man-nen keeps syllable closed
steps.note: Without doubling: *ma-nen → open syllable → long a (wrong). Doubling n: man-nen → closed → short a (correct).
Same rule. Double t prevents the syllable from opening.

steps.hint: What would *ma-nen do to the vowel?
What happens to vowel length if syllable opens?

steps.title: Long vowel + ending → drop one
steps.desc: Double-vowel word (aa, ee, oo, uu) + ending → syllable opens → one letter is enough.
goals: Know WHY a vowel letter drops

steps.note: maan = aa because closed. Adding -en opens: ma-nen. Open = long, so one a is enough.
oo → o when syllable opens.

steps.hint: What does an open syllable tell you?
Double vowel + ending → open → one letter
ee + en → open → one e enough

steps.q: Doubling consonants and dropping vowels are…
opts: Two sides of the same coin
steps.ans: Two sides of the same coin
steps.hint: One protects short, the other adjusts long

steps.title: Which rule?
steps.desc: Base word + ending: double the consonant or drop a vowel?

steps.hint: Long vowel → open syllable handles it

stages.title: Digraph Vowels & Diphthongs
stages.desc: ie, oe, eu, ui: and the spelling splits

steps.title: Three special vowel digraphs
steps.desc: ie = /i/, oe = /u/, eu = /ø/. Inherently long. Never doubled.

steps.note: ie is the long partner of short i. A digraph. Never write ii.
oe = /u/. A unique Dutch digraph. Never doubled.
eu = /ø/. No English equivalent. Shape lips for 'oh', say 'ay'.

steps.hint: ie = long version of short i

steps.q: Why never write 'ii' for long i?
steps.hint: ie is already the complete long-i digraph

steps.title: Gliding vowels
steps.desc: Three diphthongs: ei/ij, ui, ou/au. Each is ONE syllable. Two have competing spellings.

steps.note: ui = diphthong /œy/. Round lips, glide forward. Only one spelling for this sound.
ei and ij both = /ɛi/. No rule predicts which. Learn per word. Decoding is the same either way.

steps.hint: ui is a gliding vowel, not a simple vowel

steps.q: 'ei' in 'klein' and 'ij' in 'vrij': do they sound…
'ou' in 'oud' and 'au' in 'blauw': do they sound…
How do you know whether to write ei or ij?
opts: ei for short, ij for long
ij at start, ei at end
There is no rule: learn per word
ei for nouns, ij for verbs
steps.ans: There is no rule: learn per word
steps.hint: This is where Dutch spelling becomes lexical

stages.title: Irregularities & Final Review
stages.desc: Devoicing, spelling splits, and putting it all together

steps.title: Why spelling and sound disagree
steps.desc: At word end, Dutch voices consonants become voiceless. d sounds like t, v like f, z like s. But spelling keeps the original.

steps.note: d → t at word end. But the plural honden shows the d. Spelling follows the underlying consonant, not the surface sound.

steps.example: Ends in f-sound but related form: brieven → v
steps.note: v → f at word end. Plural brieven reveals the v. Spelling tracks the underlying form.

steps.q: 'hond' (dog) sounds like 'hont' at the end. Why is it spelled with d?
steps.hint: Check the plural to find the real consonant

steps.q: 'bed' sounds like 'bet'. How do you confirm the spelling?
'huis' ends in s-sound. The plural 'huizen' reveals…
steps.hint: Plural brings back the voiced consonant

steps.q: The strategy for spelling word-final consonants is…
opts: Listen carefully and write what you hear
Find a related form where the consonant is not final
steps.ans: Find a related form where the consonant is not final

steps.title: Past tense: -te or -de?
steps.desc: Weak verbs: stem ends in t/k/f/s/ch/p → -te. Otherwise → -de. The mnemonic: 't kofschip.
goals: Watch out for final devoicing masking stems

steps.example: werk → stem ends in k → 't kofschip → -te
steps.note: The stem 'werk' ends in k. k is in 't kofschip (t, k, f, s, ch, p) → past tense = -te.

steps.example: leev- → stem ends in v (not in 't kofschip) → -de
steps.exampleEn: Watch out: sounds like f but stem has v
steps.note: The stem is leev- (leven). v is NOT in 't kofschip → -de. But at word end v → f, so 'leefde' has f in spelling. Tricky!

steps.hint: Is k in 't kofschip (t, k, f, s, ch, p)?
Does it end in a 't kofschip letter?

steps.q: Why is final devoicing dangerous for 't kofschip?
opts: It can mask the real stem consonant
steps.ans: It can mask the real stem consonant
steps.hint: If v sounds like f at word end, which rule applies?

steps.title: Putting it all together
steps.desc: A final review across everything: vowels, syllables, spelling rules, digraphs, devoicing.

steps.q: In 'ma-ken' (to make), the first syllable is open. The vowel is…
Which contains the digraph vowel oe?
'geld' (money) sounds like 'gelt'. The d is confirmed by…
A pseudoword 'blaan': you have never seen this word. Based on Dutch rules, the vowel is…
opts: Short because it is one syllable
Short because bl- is a cluster
steps.hint: What does a double vowel always mean?

steps.q: A pseudoword 'trom': based on rules, the vowel is…
steps.hint: Single vowel + consonant ending = closed syllable = ?

stages.title: Stage 1: Script Basics
stages.desc: How Arabic text works: direction, shapes, and dots

steps.title: Arabic reads right to left
steps.desc: Unlike English, Arabic text starts on the right side and flows to the left. Numbers, however, are still read left to right.
goals: Know that numbers go left to right

steps.q: Which direction does Arabic text flow?
You open an Arabic book. Where does the first word appear?

steps.title: Arabic is cursive
steps.desc: Every Arabic letter connects to the next (except 6 special letters). The same letter looks different depending on where it appears in a word: beginning, middle, or end.
goals: Know letters change shape by position

steps.q: Arabic letters in a word are:
How many shape forms can an Arabic letter have?
The 4 forms of a letter depend on:
Arabic cursive means that most letters:

steps.title: Dots are part of the letter
steps.desc: Many Arabic letters share the same base shape. Dots above or below tell you WHICH letter it is. Changing the dots changes the letter entirely.
goals: Same shape + different dots = different letter

steps.q: Two Arabic letters have the same base shape. What makes them different?
If you remove a dot from a letter, you get:

steps.title: Six letters that break the chain
steps.desc: Most Arabic letters connect on both sides. But 6 letters (called breakers) ONLY connect to the letter before them — never to the letter after. The next letter has to restart.

steps.q: How many 'breaker' letters exist in Arabic?
After a breaker letter, the next letter:

stages.title: Stage 2: First Letters
stages.desc: Learn your first Arabic letters — one at a time, sound first.

steps.title: Three shapes, one base
steps.desc: These three letters all look the same — only the dots are different. Learn the sound each one makes.
goals: Learn the sounds ba, ta, tha
Tell them apart by their dots

steps.note: This is one of the most common Arabic letters.
Same shape as ba, but with 2 dots above.
Same shape again, but with 3 dots above.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ب
What sound does this letter make?  ت
What sound does this letter make?  ث

steps.title: A bowl, a tail, and a tall line
steps.desc: These three letters each have a unique shape. One of them is special — it breaks the chain.
goals: Learn the sounds nun, ya, alif
Know that alif is a breaker

steps.note: Don't confuse with ba — nun's dot is ABOVE a bowl, ba's dot is BELOW a teeth shape.
Also used as the long 'ee' vowel sound.

steps.exampleEn: No dots — this is a BREAKER letter
steps.note: Alif never connects forward. It is one of the 6 breaker letters.
steps.formNote: Only 2 forms — breakers never connect to the next letter.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ن
What sound does this letter make?  ي
This letter is a breaker:  ا
How many forms does alif have?

steps.title: The most common connector
steps.desc: Lam appears in almost every Arabic sentence. It is part of the word for 'the' — the most frequent word in Arabic.

steps.exampleEn: Tall curved stroke — connects both sides
steps.note: Part of ال (al = 'the'). You will see this letter everywhere.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ل
Which common Arabic word contains lam?

steps.title: The round loop
steps.desc: Mim is a small round shape. Like lam, it connects on both sides and appears very frequently.

steps.exampleEn: Small round loop — connects both sides
steps.note: Don't confuse with nun. Mim has no dots. Nun has 1 dot above.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  م
How is mim different from nun?

stages.title: Stage 3: More Letters
stages.desc: Learn the remaining Arabic letters — breakers, families, and unique sounds.

steps.title: Letters that break the chain
steps.desc: You already know alif and waw are breakers. Here are the other four. All 6 breakers only connect to the letter before them — never forward.
goals: Learn dal, dhal, ra, zay, waw

steps.note: Breaker: only connects to the letter before it. 2 forms only.
steps.formNote: Breakers only have isolated and final forms.

steps.exampleEn: Same hook as dal + 1 dot above
steps.note: Same shape as dal, but with a dot above. Also a breaker.
Breaker. Different shape from dal — ra swoops down smoothly.

steps.exampleEn: Same swoop as ra + 1 dot above
steps.note: Same shape as ra, but with a dot above. Also a breaker.
Breaker. Also doubles as the long vowel 'oo'.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  د
What sound does this letter make?  ر
What sound does this letter make?  و
How many breaker letters are there in total?
Breaker letters only have how many forms?
Adding a dot above د changes the sound to:

steps.title: Teeth rows and a new connector
steps.desc: Sin and shin look like a row of 3 small teeth. Kaf is a new shape that connects on both sides.
goals: Tell sin and shin apart by dots

steps.exampleEn: 3 small teeth in a row — no dots
steps.note: Looks like a zigzag or 3 small bumps.
Same base as sin but with 3 dots above.
A tall letter with a small stroke inside.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  س
What sound does this letter make?  ش
What sound does this letter make?  ك
Sin and shin share the same base shape. How do you tell them apart?

steps.title: Three letters, one bowl shape
steps.desc: Jim, breathy ha, and kha all share a bowl shape. Only the dots differ — just like the teeth family.
goals: Tell them apart by dot placement

steps.note: The dot is BELOW the bowl.

steps.example: A breathy 'h' from the throat — no English equivalent
steps.note: The only bowl-family letter with no dots at all.
Same bowl as breathy ha, but with a dot ABOVE.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ج
What sound does this letter make?  ح
What sound does this letter make?  خ

steps.title: Four emphatic sounds unique to Arabic
steps.desc: These letters have heavier, deeper sounds than their light counterparts. Two pairs: flat base and tall loop.
goals: Learn sad, dad, heavy ta, heavy dha
Know these are the emphatic letters

steps.note: The heavy version of sin.
Arabic is sometimes called 'the language of dad' because this sound is unique to it.
The heavy version of regular ta.
The heavy version of dhal.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ص
What sound does this letter make?  ض
What sound does this letter make?  ط
What sound does this letter make?  ظ
Which letter is unique to Arabic?

steps.title: The shape-shifter and the throat letters
steps.desc: Ha changes shape dramatically. Ayn and ghayn are throat sounds — ayn has no English equivalent.
goals: Know ayn is a throat squeeze

steps.exampleEn: Changes shape a lot in different positions
steps.note: Not the same as breathy ha. This is a regular 'h' but its shape changes a lot.

steps.example: A squeezed throat sound — no English equivalent
steps.note: One of the most important Arabic sounds. Made by tightening the throat.

steps.exampleEn: Same loop as ayn + 1 dot above
steps.note: Same shape as ayn but with a dot above.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ه
What sound does this letter make?  ع
What sound does this letter make?  غ
Ayn and ghayn share the same shape. How do you tell them apart?

steps.title: The cup pair — last two letters!
steps.desc: Fa and qaf both have a cup shape. Fa has 1 dot, qaf has 2. These are the last two letters!

steps.note: A familiar sound with a simple shape.

steps.example: A deep 'k' sound from the back of the throat
steps.note: Deeper than kaf. Made further back in the throat.

steps.q: What sound does this letter make?  ف
What sound does this letter make?  ق
Fa and qaf share the same shape. How do you tell them apart?
You now know all 28 Arabic letters! How many are breakers?

stages.title: Stage 4: Vowel Marks
stages.desc: The short vowels, silence mark, and doubling

steps.title: Arabic short vowels are small marks
steps.desc: Arabic mainly writes consonants. Short vowels are shown as tiny marks above or below letters. There are 3: fatha (a), damma (u), kasra (i).
goals: Know they go above or below consonants

steps.q: Short vowels in Arabic are written as:
How many short vowel marks does Arabic have?
The short vowel mark for the 'a' sound is placed:
The short vowel mark for the 'i' sound is placed:
The short vowel mark for the 'u' sound is placed:

steps.title: Silence and doubling
steps.desc: Sukun (a small circle above) means NO vowel after this consonant. Shadda (a small w-shape above) means the consonant is DOUBLED — say it twice.

steps.q: A small circle above a letter means:
A small w-shape (shadda) above a letter means:
If you see sukun on ب, it means:
If you see shadda on ت, it means:

steps.title: The -n endings and long vowels
steps.desc: Tanwin adds an 'n' sound to a vowel ending: -an, -un, -in. Long vowels use letters (ا for aa, و for oo, ي for ee) instead of marks.

steps.q: Tanwin turns a short vowel into:

steps.title: Combining consonants and vowels
steps.desc: An open syllable is: consonant + vowel mark (like بَ = ba). A closed syllable adds a sukun consonant at the end (like بَتْ = bat).

steps.q: An open syllable in Arabic is:
What type of syllable is: consonant + fatha?
What type of syllable is: consonant + fatha + consonant with sukun?

stages.title: Stage 5: Real Text
stages.desc: Prefixes, special combos, look-alikes, and your first words

steps.title: The most common prefix
steps.desc: ال (alif + lam) means 'the' in Arabic. It attaches to the front of nouns. You will see it everywhere in Arabic text.

steps.q: ال at the start of a word means:
ال is made of which two letters?
Where does ال appear in a word?

steps.title: A mandatory merge and Arabic digits
steps.desc: When ل and ا appear together, they ALWAYS merge into لا (laa). Also: Arabic uses its own digit shapes ٠١٢٣٤٥٦٧٨٩.
goals: Recognize لا as one combined shape

steps.q: When ل and ا appear next to each other, they:
Arabic uses its own shapes for:

opts: Left to right like Western numbers
steps.ans: Left to right like Western numbers

steps.title: Letters that look almost identical
steps.desc: ة and ه look alike but ة has 2 dots. ى and ي look alike but ي has 2 dots below. Mixing them up changes the meaning.

opts: ي has 2 dots below but ى has none
steps.ans: ي has 2 dots below but ى has none

steps.q: If you see dots below at the end of a word, it is:

steps.title: Reading real Arabic words
steps.desc: You now know all 28 letters, all vowel marks, the prefix ال, and common look-alikes. Time to put it all together with the shortest Arabic words.

steps.q: You can now decode Arabic script! How many letters does the Arabic alphabet have?
